story:
Small towns advertise their secrets if you know where to look. In Landover it wasn’t snarling dogs or shuttered porches; it was children’s things without children: a red ball in the grass, a jump rope on the playground, chalk shattered on a sidewalk. I stopped for the night because I was too tired to drive on. The only thing open was a rundown eight-room motel. The manager, a wiry Black woman with milk-rimmed eyes and a cobweb of white hair, watched me sign the book, then later clamped my hand when I asked where the kids were. “There aren’t any. Not anymore.” She added a proverb I didn’t understand: if you don’t listen, you end up in the place for those who won’t heed advice. It felt like a warning and a dare.

I stayed. Curiosity isn’t something I turn off. I watched the school bus make a full route to an empty school and back to a silent depot. No child boarded. A few staff cars sat in their spots, but the halls were dead. I followed two teachers home and saw them drink on their porch, normal as you please. Still no children, anywhere. Only strollers with no babies, candy wrappers scudding in the grass, tiny sneaker prints in dirt that led nowhere. The absence pressed on my chest like weather. If there had been kids “not anymore,” what had changed?

On my last night I pushed. In a bar called Wednesdays, I asked the mustached bartender where the kids were. A voice rolled out from the corner—deep, confident. A bald man in mirrored shades leaned forward, three white-haired women in black standing behind him like a chorus. “Who’s asking?” he said, then, as I backed off, “See you soon.” A skittering sound followed. A stain on the floor shifted like a shadow with no owner. I left.

In the parking lot, an old Fiat sat with something moving inside it. My car fob failed, my fingers fumbled the key, and a door on the Fiat creaked open. I didn’t wait around. I drove hard through dark with no streetlights, afraid my imagination wasn’t imagining enough.

Back at the motel, my door gaped. My bedside lamp had been on; now the room was dark. When I stepped inside, the lights flared and the room filled with faces from the day: the bus driver, the teachers, the bartender’s regulars. On the bed lay the contents of my bag: handcuffs, a scalpel, a bone saw, everything except the apron I used when I did the things I never confessed. Their reflection in the metal told me the truth I thought I’d hidden—what I had done and what I had come to do.

The manager stepped from the bathroom wearing my black rubber apron. She slid off her wig. Bald. The same skull as the man in the bar. The same grin. Hands pinned my arms. She lifted my scalpel and made sure I watched. “We protect,” she said softly. “We do not sharpen the stick that would like to pierce our eye. We uproot it.” The three white-haired women spoke together: “When you poison others, some touches your mouth.”

Only then did I understand. The abandoned toys, the bus that collected no one, the perfect little prints—bait. Landover wasn’t empty of children by accident. It was a trap for people like me. They had seen through my curiosity to the hunger beneath it, the guilt I kept buried, the harm I intended. The scalpel found my throat. The pain was bright, and the voice at my ear was colder than the blade: “You won’t be doing those things. Not to any children. Not anymore.”
